perturbation.original_text: Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore at no cost to Distributor only if such cancellation or modification request is made within [*****] business days of PO acceptance or rescheduling request is made at least [*****] business days prior to the most current Estimated Shipping Date for that purchase order. Distributor has the right to change the destination of all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore at no cost to Distributor only if such change in destination request is made at least [*****] business days prior to the most current Estimated Shipping Date for that purchase order. Except as set forth above, purchase orders are non-changeable and non- cancellable by Distributor, once accepted by Xplore. Certain purchase orders, determined at Xplore's sole discretion, and generally of large volume and/or extended lead times, may be subject to alternative rescheduling, cancellation, and modification rights. Should such purchase orders be subject to alternative rights, Xplore shall inform Distributor of alternative rights prior to order acceptance. Distributor will then have [*****] business days to accept, or reject, the alternative terms of Xplore for that certain purchase order. If Distributor rejects such alternative terms, the purchase order will not be accepted by Xplore.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore. Except as set forth above, purchase orders are non-changeable and non- cancellable by Distributor, once accepted by Xplore. Certain purchase orders, determined at Xplore's sole discretion, and generally of large volume and/or extended lead times, may be subject to alternative rescheduling, cancellation, and modification rights. Should such purchase orders be subject to alternative rights, Xplore shall inform Distributor of alternative rights prior to order acceptance. Distributor will then have [*****] business days to accept, or reject, the alternative terms of Xplore for that certain purchase order. If Distributor rejects such alternative terms, the purchase order will not be accepted by Xplore.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific conditions under which a distributor can cancel/reschedule/modify a purchase order, the section becomes internally contradictory. It initially states the distributor 'has the right' but omits the timeframe for said rights. Then, it states purchase orders are 'non-changeable and non-cancellable', creating an in-text contradiction in Article I, Section 2.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Purchase Order Rescheduling, Cancellation and Modification. Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore. Except as set forth above, purchase orders are non-changeable and non- cancellable by Distributor, once accepted by Xplore. Certain purchase orders, determined at Xplore's sole discretion, and generally of large volume and/or extended lead times, may be subject to alternative rescheduling, cancellation, and modification rights. Should such purchase orders be subject to alternative rights, Xplore shall inform Distributor of alternative rights prior to order acceptance. Distributor will then have [*****] business days to accept, or reject, the alternative terms of Xplore for that certain purchase order. If Distributor rejects such alternative terms, the purchase order will not be accepted by Xplore.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified conditions for the distributor's right to cancel, reschedule, or modify a purchase order. The changed text removes these conditions, stating the distributor 'has the right' without any limitations, while simultaneously stating that purchase orders are 'non-changeable and non-cancellable.' This creates a direct contradiction within Article I, Section 2 (as described in the explanation) because the changed text now states the distributor *both* can and cannot change/cancel orders, making the contract ambiguous and unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales. Xplore acknowledges that from time to time, Distributor's inventory levels may fall below the thirty (30) days goal that is agreed upon by both Parties. If inventory levels fall below the thirty (30) day goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales. Xplore acknowledges that from time to time, Distributor's inventory levels may fall below that is agreed upon by both Parties. If inventory levels fall below the goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the specific 'thirty (30) days' quantity, it creates ambiguity and subsequently contradictions. The initial sentence speaks about distributors commercially reasonable efforts to maintain stock. Then, Xplore says that distributor's inventory levels may fall below that is agreed upon by both Parties, not making sense because it does not specify the amount agreed. This creates an in-text contradiction because it references an inventory target that's no longer explicitly defined in Article I, Section 4.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Stock on Hand. Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales. Xplore acknowledges that from time to time, Distributor's inventory levels may fall below that is agreed upon by both Parties. If inventory levels fall below the goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties.
perturbation.justification: The omission of 'thirty (30) days' from the changed text creates a contradiction. The original text clearly defines a specific inventory level target. The changed text removes this specific target, but the contradicted text still refers to 'that is agreed upon by both Parties,' which now lacks a clear referent, leading to ambiguity and making the fulfillment of Xplore's obligation to replenish stock unclear, directly impacting enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The terms of Section 3 of Schedule 2 of the Distribution Agreement will apply to Xplore Products, provided however that stock rotation allowance for Xplore Products will be based on the net dollar value of Distributor's purchases in each calendar quarter of Xplore Products and such allowance will be calculated separate and apart from all other Products purchased by Distributor during such period.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms of the Distribution Agreement will apply to Xplore Products.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the provision of 'stock rotation allowance' a key clause is removed, but the whole Article is still referencing to it. This omission makes the enforcement of the provision of products vague and create an in-text contradiction on what terms are referring to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Product Return and Stock Rotation. The terms of the Distribution Agreement will apply to Xplore Products.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that 'stock rotation allowance' will be based on net dollar value of purchases and calculated separately. The changed text removes this specific provision, stating only that 'the terms of the Distribution Agreement will apply to Xplore Products'. The contradicted text references 'Product Return and Stock Rotation' and also states 'The terms of the Distribution Agreement will apply to Xplore Products.' By removing the provision for 'stock rotation allowance', the changed text creates an omission, as defined in the Perturbation Definitions, making the application of terms related to stock rotation ambiguous and contradictory, since the specific calculation method has been deleted from the primary reference. This lack of clarity, directly arising from the omission, creates an in-text contradiction.
